questions.question: What does `fscanf()` essentially do?
options: Reads from a file pointer and attempts to match the text to complex format specifiers (like %d, %s) instantly converting them
questions.answer: Reads from a file pointer and attempts to match the text to complex format specifiers (like %d, %s) instantly converting them

questions.question: What is the primary danger of using `%s` in `fscanf`?
options: If the text in the file is longer than your array, it causes a Buffer Overflow, destroying adjacent memory
questions.answer: If the text in the file is longer than your array, it causes a Buffer Overflow, destroying adjacent memory

questions.question: How do you securely limit `fscanf` to reading exactly 19 characters into a string?
Under what condition does `fscanf` immediately stop reading a `%s` string token?
options: When it hits any whitespace character (space, tab, or newline)
questions.answer: When it hits any whitespace character (space, tab, or newline)

questions.question: What does `sscanf()` accomplish?
options: It functions identically to fscanf, but it reads text from a standard string variable/buffer you provide instead of from a physical file.
questions.answer: It functions identically to fscanf, but it reads text from a standard string variable/buffer you provide instead of from a physical file.

questions.question: If you `sscanf("Apple 5", "%s %d", fruit, count);`, what is required to correctly save the `count` integer?
options: You must use `&count` to pass the pointer/address so sscanf can successfully write the integer to your variable.
questions.answer: You must use `&count` to pass the pointer/address so sscanf can successfully write the integer to your variable.

questions.question: Why don't you need the `&` operator for the string `fruit` in `sscanf`?
options: Because `fruit` is an Array, and array names naturally naturally decay into memory address pointers automatically.
questions.answer: Because `fruit` is an Array, and array names naturally naturally decay into memory address pointers automatically.

questions.question: What does `fscanf` return?
options: The number of items/tokens it successfully managed to match and assign variables to
questions.answer: The number of items/tokens it successfully managed to match and assign variables to

questions.question: What happens if `fscanf` hits the end of the file before matching anything?
options: It returns the special constant integer EOF
questions.answer: It returns the special constant integer EOF

questions.question: Which function allows securely dumping a formatted string completely into a memory buffer instead of printing it to a screen?
questions.answer: sprintf() / snprintf()

sublevels.theory:
**Struct Serialization**
If you have an array of 1000 Structs representing items in a game, reading/writing them manually as text (`"Item Name: Sword, ATK: 50\n"`) is excruciatingly slow and takes massive disk space.

Instead, you use **Binary Serialization** with `"wb"` modes.
`fwrite(&inventory, sizeof(struct Item), 1000, fptr);`
This single line takes all 1000 structs in RAM, completely bypasses text translation, and physically blasts the raw binary bytes directly onto the hard drive in milliseconds.

To load the entire game state back on your next program run:
`fread(&inventory, sizeof(struct Item), 1000, fptr);`

questions.question: What is 'Serialization'?
options: Converting complex data structures in RAM (like Structs) into a linear format (like raw bytes or text) so it can be saved to a file or sent over a network
questions.answer: Converting complex data structures in RAM (like Structs) into a linear format (like raw bytes or text) so it can be saved to a file or sent over a network

questions.question: What is the incredible advantage of Binary Serialization via `fwrite` over Text Files?
options: It is extraordinarily fast and disk-efficient since the CPU doesn't waste time translating binary integers back-and-forth into readable string characters
questions.answer: It is extraordinarily fast and disk-efficient since the CPU doesn't waste time translating binary integers back-and-forth into readable string characters

questions.question: If you open a file for `fwrite` binary structs, what mode must you definitely use?
questions.answer: "wb"

questions.question: What does the '3rd argument' in `fwrite` dictate: `fwrite(data, size, COUNT, file)`?
options: The number of individual structural elements (chunks of 'size') you want to write sequentially in one swoop
questions.answer: The number of individual structural elements (chunks of 'size') you want to write sequentially in one swoop

questions.question: Can you safely `fwrite` an array of structs containing strings defined as `char name[50];`?
options: Yes! Because `name` is a fixed array INSIDE the struct, the 50 bytes of characters physically live right there and get written successfully.
Only if it is null terminated
questions.answer: Yes! Because `name` is a fixed array INSIDE the struct, the 50 bytes of characters physically live right there and get written successfully.

questions.question: Can you safely `fwrite` an array of structs containing strongly dynamic Pointers `char *name;`?
options: Never! The struct only holds the Memory Address. You will write a useless hex address to disk, while the actual string string data located elsewhere in RAM is totally lost.
questions.answer: Never! The struct only holds the Memory Address. You will write a useless hex address to disk, while the actual string string data located elsewhere in RAM is totally lost.

questions.question: What happens if a hacker opens your `"wb"` binary `.dat` file in a simple Text editor?
options: It appears as absolute unintelligible gibberish and weird symbols because the text editor is attempting to decode pure mathematical binary values as ASCII characters.
questions.answer: It appears as absolute unintelligible gibberish and weird symbols because the text editor is attempting to decode pure mathematical binary values as ASCII characters.

questions.question: What prevents a binary `.dat` file saved by a C program on a Windows computer from loading perfectly into a C program running on a Mac/Linux computer?
options: Struct Padding rules and Endianness (byte order) can drastically differ between compilers and completely different CPU Architectures, corrupting the un-formatted struct alignment
questions.answer: Struct Padding rules and Endianness (byte order) can drastically differ between compilers and completely different CPU Architectures, corrupting the un-formatted struct alignment

questions.question: If you `fread` 100 structs from a file, but the file only contains 40, what does `fread` return?
options: It returns the number 40 (the amount of items successfully fully read)
questions.answer: It returns the number 40 (the amount of items successfully fully read)

questions.question: How do you append newly created binary structs safely to an already populated `.dat` file?
options: Read it all, then rewrite it
questions.answer: Open in "ab" (Append Binary Mode)

sublevels.theory:
**Memory Mapping (`mmap`)**
For absolute maximum performance on Unix systems, you don't even use `fread/fwrite`. You use POSIX Memory Mapping.

`mmap()` asks the OS kernel to magically link a file on your Hard Drive directly into your program's internal RAM address space. You receive a standard Pointer. 
If you write to `ptr[5] = 'A'`, it natively modifies the Hard Drive file transparently in the background! It bypasses all standard C copying buffers entirely.

**File Descriptors**
Under underneath the friendly `FILE *` pointer from C `stdio`, the underlying Operating System purely relies on **File Descriptors**: simple integers (like `3`, `4`, `5`). You can access OS-level raw system calls bypassing C standard library by using `read()` and `write()` with these ints directly.

questions.question: What incredibly advanced concept physically maps a hard drive file transparently into your RAM?
questions.answer: mmap() (Memory Mapping)

questions.question: Why is `mmap()` significantly faster than standard `fread/fwrite` for gigantic files (e.g., 10GB databases)?
options: It completely bypasses creating temporary copying buffers in standard RAM; you read/write exactly directly to the OS disk cache
questions.answer: It completely bypasses creating temporary copying buffers in standard RAM; you read/write exactly directly to the OS disk cache

questions.question: Behind the scenes of `<stdio.h>`, what does the OS (like Linux/Unix) actually use to uniquely identify an open file?
questions.answer: A simple 'File Descriptor' Integer (e.g. 3, 4, 5)

questions.question: When your C program launches on Linux, what File Descriptors are automatically pre-opened at integers 0, 1, and 2?
options: Standard Input (stdin), Standard Output (stdout), and Standard Error (stderr)
questions.answer: Standard Input (stdin), Standard Output (stdout), and Standard Error (stderr)

questions.question: If you want to extract the underlying OS File Descriptor int from a C `FILE *` pointer, what Unix function do you use?
questions.answer: fileno(fptr)

questions.question: What is the raw POSIX System Call for writing bytes directly to a File Descriptor?
questions.answer: write()

questions.question: What is 'File Locking' (`flock` / `fcntl`)?
options: Requesting the OS block any other programs from simultaneously modifying a given file while you are writing to it, preventing horrible race condition corruption
questions.answer: Requesting the OS block any other programs from simultaneously modifying a given file while you are writing to it, preventing horrible race condition corruption

questions.question: What happens if two totally separate programs furiously write to the exact same log file concurrently without File Locking?
options: Their textual outputs violently interleave together mid-sentence, permanently corrupting the log file data layout
questions.answer: Their textual outputs violently interleave together mid-sentence, permanently corrupting the log file data layout

questions.question: How do you permanently rename or move a file strictly using C standard libraries?
questions.answer: rename("old.txt", "new.txt")

questions.question: What does `remove("data.txt")` do?
options: Permanently deletes the file from your Hard Drive
questions.answer: Permanently deletes the file from your Hard Drive
